speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about the Pinto famous legacy with the Sephardic. Would you just tell us a little bit more what you know about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all I know about them is when I got that letter from that lawyer, the history of my family going to America, coming to Holland. That was in 1492, so, so, so many years ago. And I have it all-- the whole history, I have here. And the whole family, my father's side is all Sephardic. My grandmother's name is Cardozo. My grandfather, Jacob Jessurun Pinto, I'm named after. And [? Messias ?] is my family in London, all Sephardic, Fernandez. They think we're Mexicans. We're Sephardic-- Fernandez, [INAUDIBLE]. the They all died, this family. Every one of them died. There's still some cousins living there who are still. I was in the choir in London in the Spanish and Portuguese synagogue in Bevis Marks in Ams-- in London. As a little boy, I was in the choir. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask-- these events through your life, your experiences, have they changed your feelings about religion in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I love my religion-- nothing. I'm Jewish. And I will stay Jewish. And I love it. I like to go to shul. But at the moment, I'm now a member of this Orthodox [INAUDIBLE]. But it's so far away, it's impossible to walk. It's, it's four mile-- three miles for me. I can't do that. The rabbi was on my [INAUDIBLE] and the [INAUDIBLE]. Cause the Sephardic here is on Wilshire. Now, I hear it's-- there's a lot of trouble there on Wilshire.

speaker: interviewer
text: You shared so many times your experiences. Why do you feel the importance of sharing these experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: The world to know, my grandson to know the details [INAUDIBLE] further. When I spoke in his school, with the class, with the children, when the children wrote me those letters and the questions what they were asking me. They've sent me thank you, thank you cards for coming to the school and speak. And they experience what I've done.

speaker: interviewer
text: I get a call from a father from the Lutheran church, [INAUDIBLE]. Jack, I have a friend. He's a Jewish man. He told me about you. Will you come to our service and speak to the people on Sunday? I said, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he picked me up here. [? There was this. ?] And there was a podium. Lutheran is something like Catholic, but it's not real Catholic. It's-- but the story of what I knew from Luther, and he was a, he was a bad man. I have the history. But they are ashamed of him.  That's what he told me. Then I spoke with the congregation. They were black there, Mexicans, and American people. They cried. When I was finished, they came to the podium. And they hug me and kiss me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we know it's true. Why are people saying it wasn't true? We're going to talk. Everywhere we go, we'll talk about what they done. Why did Lutheran do that, cut the tongues out? They know it. I have the history of Luther, the history of the Jewish book, the Jewish people. But now, they're changing, the Lutherans. Now, I've had a from the Catholic church. They want me to come and speak. I had one time, in Culver City, two nuns come on the door. Why I don't go to church? I said, 'Why?' Pinto, you're, you're Spanish. I'm Jewish, I said. They, they wouldn't believe it. So I told them of the Inquisition. Spanish name, Portuguese name-- and then I gave them something. They went away. But I love my religion so much. You never questioned it at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why this was being done because you were a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the only thing. Why? Why? Why? The whole history-- when you go back to the history, the story of the Jews, how it started, the history of the Jew, in every country, there was massacres and massacres-- in France, in Germany, everywhere. If the Jews would not, not have been massacred, we would have just so many Jews as Chinese in the world. But they were massacred. Why? What did they do? Because they're Jewish? They were children playing on the street. They just murdered them. They say the matzo is made from blood and all that kind of stuff and all. And it's still going on. It's still going on. It won't stop. That's why the world has to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're giving you this chance to talk to the world and all your future generations. What do you want to tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell them why the hate to the Jews? They don't do no harm. They're very educated people. The children are very educated. The parents, when they came to America, were shipped on crates just to give their children education.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know there's a lot of lawyers and doctor, But the normal Jewish people also They go to shuls, but a lot of them are mixed marriages. That's too bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I've got to tell you something. All my friends who were in the concentration camp, only [INAUDIBLE] I know. But the rest, all of the children are married with goyim, with chi-- Christian children, with Catholic and Protestant and do nothing with the Jewish, nothing with the Jewish religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you fear for the Jewish survival?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, worries only-- I not fear. I know with my son and my grandson, he learns-- he's learning for his bar mitzvah. And he's proud of it. He's a good boy. And he gave his first seder-- that was last seder at my son's house. And he's so a good boy. And he knows what I went through. He said later, we'll show it. When you're not there, this has to be shown to the world. Now that's [INAUDIBLE],

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the 'Alexander's Ragtime Band' as almost saving your life or perhaps saving-- have saved your life for it. When you hear that song today, or you sing that song today, what do you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: My memory goes back. I know the words. I've sang it  here for the city folk. I sang it in the hospital. For the cancer organization, I sing. And then I sing to my wife every day, 'I Don't Know Why I Love You Like I Do.' You know the song?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sing, sing a little bit for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING] I don't know why I love you like I do. I don't know why, why I do. I don't know why he loves you like you do. I don't know why, why you do. You never seem to love my romancing. Oh, baby, I wish you'd stop with me and start we dancing. I don't know why, you [? lovely ?] [? like ?] you do. I don't know why--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then Alexander Band? [SINGING] Come on and hear, come on and hear, Alexander's Ragtime Band. Come on and hear, come on and hear! It's the best band in the land. And they can play the bugle call, like you've never heard before. So naturally that they think you're going to war. Who's the best band in that land? Oh, honey land, come on along. Come on along, and let us join up with the band.

speaker: interviewee
text: Come along, come along, and let us sing like the band. You can hear the Swanee River played by Ragtime. Come on along, come on along, and listen to the Alexander's Ragtime Band!

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful. We'd like you to share some of the photographs with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything before that you'd like to tell on this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The most things I've told the history from the picture and the history from the soldiers who saved my life, who came up in the, in, in the hospital. God has taken care of me. Believe me, that what I went through, my legs. Just two friends have died last week, this week, the same day who live here. They're American-born. And they live here. They both died this week. We have two funerals this week-- Shirley Roth and Marty, so nice people.

speaker: interviewer
text: We've been friends [? for long. ?] And they died. And now they're going to be buried in Eden, where we are going to be. You know Eden? That's where we're being buried. Well, we thank you for taking part in this, Jack.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we want to, we want to share it with now with some of the things that you have that are important.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my mother. Her name is Alida, A-L-I-D-A, Preso, P-R-E-S-O. That was her maiden name. My father's name was Joseph Jessurun Pinto-- Joseph, you remember, Jessurun Pinto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in England. They were younger then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to the-- to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- when I was taken away, I tried to get in contact. But I never saw them. I never saw them again. The little girl is my daughter. Here name  is Sientje Jessurun Pinto. And the other one is my son. His name is Joseph Jessurun Pinto, but we call him [? Cinche, ?] [? Sine ?] and Johnny.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was Sientje born? April-- April 27, 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sientje. And Johnny?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 20th September, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went further when we were in the train. They pulled me out. They were sitting on my lap. And then they pulled me out. And I was holding my wife's hand. They pulled me out. And she screams. And they start crying. One hit me. And Sientje said, 'Daddy, daddy, daddy!' And then the train went away. I never saw them back again anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in November of 1940-- I mean, October, 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That is my wife, Ella Ziff. Her maiden name was Ziff. And that's me, Ja-Jacob Jessurun Pinto. That's my original birth certificate name. We were married on the 27th of September, 1936, in the Spanish and Portuguese Synagogue in Amsterdam. The dress what she's wearing is made by my sister who was a well-known dressmaker. And then two years later, we got the children. My wife w-worked in a department store, store those two years. And I worked as a barber.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to Ella?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was taken away together with the children on the train, the three of them, crying. And I never saw them back again. That was the last I saw of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was also taken to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with the children together. There were four of us. And I was pulled, pulled out of the train. And they went further. What happened? I don't know. Went right to the gas chamber? The Red Cross paper says yes, in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a banquet party from the City of Hope, what we have once a year for the donors. I'm standing on the right. My son, the tall one is standing next to me, Maurice, my daughter-in-law, Rochelle, my grandson, Daniel, and my wife, Dora. This was taken last September, yes, when we had the annual party.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a picture. When I got to know my wife, we made plans to marry. Went to her house. I rented this house. It was all filthy, of course.  It had to be cleaned. All the wallpaper-- was usually in Amsterdam, everybody has wallpaper. We tore it away. And behind the wallpaper, there was still a wooden panel. And we tore that away. And this picture fell, fell behind the wall. We're sure it was a kind of tapestry. I said to my wife, those people, the Jewish people taken away. And they hide this picture. So we went to the Jewish organization in Amsterdam and showed them. They said, you, you can't have it. We have to save it. Somebody can claim it later. We don't know if they're living. But the law here in Holland is a year and six weeks. Nobody claims that-- it's the law of the country-- then it's yours. And nobody ever claimed it. I got a letter from the Jewish organization that I can own the picture. And that's how I got the picture. It's an unbelievable picture. The tapestry was done in that time, must have been 200 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Pinto, you can do anything you want.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Dora, for 47 years, beautiful woman who takes care of me with my sickness. She's always on my side. Thank you, Dora. This is my son, Maurice-- Very. Good for his family, does his best. He done his best in school and at his work. And we're very proud of him. And this is my daughter-in-law. They've been married 16 years-- very good for the family, especially with Daniel. She takes care of him wonderful. She does everything for him. And now he's learning the trumpet. It makes us crazy sometimes. But he's a very fine boy. We love him very much. This coming May, 1996, he will be starting to learn for his bar mitzvah. And that, we're proud of. We have a real Jewish family who we are proud of. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: The only thing is you didn't mention her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: You forgot my name, Rochelle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rochelle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rochelle, Rochella Pinto. I forgot all about it.
